title: How much does a concrete patio cost per square foot?
excerpt: A poured concrete patio costs $6-$15 per square foot installed in 2026. Broom finish sits at $6-$10, stamped lands at $10-$15.
metaKeywords: concrete patio price per square foot
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed patio, driveway, slab, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A poured concrete patio in 2026 lands between $6 and $15 per square foot installed, with the national typical near $8.50 per square foot for a 4-inch broom-finish slab on a prepared subgrade. That figure is normalized for 3,500 PSI ready-mix delivery, 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh, control joints at 8-10 foot spacing, and a single mobilization fee. Stamped patios with a single color land between $11 and $14 per square foot, and multi-color stamped finishes push to $13-$18. The single biggest swing factor on a homeowner's actual bill is access — backyards with no truck access add $1.50-$3 per square foot in pump or wheelbarrow labor.

faqs.question: What is the average cost of a concrete patio in 2026?
faqs.answer: The national average for a concrete patio in 2026 is $8.50 per square foot installed for a standard 4-inch broom-finish slab. A typical 12x16 patio (192 square feet) costs $1,150 to $2,900 installed in plain finish, and $2,100 to $3,500 in stamped. The largest variable on a backyard patio quote is access — a wheelbarrow pour adds $1.50-$3 per square foot vs a direct truck-chute pour.

faqs.question: Is a stamped concrete patio worth the extra cost?
faqs.answer: Stamped concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot over broom finish — roughly $700-$1,500 on a typical 12x16 patio. According to the Portland Cement Association, the structural performance is identical: the upcharge is entirely labor (extra crew hours during finishing), stamping mats, color hardener, and release agent. The return shows up in resale value, not in concrete performance.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete patio be?
faqs.answer: A residential concrete patio should be a minimum of 4 inches thick. Patios that will hold a hot tub, outdoor kitchen, or heavy furniture should bump to 5 inches. Each additional inch of thickness adds about $0.75-$1.25 per square foot. Going below 4 inches saves a few hundred dollars but routinely cracks within 2-5 years.

faqs.question: Do concrete patios crack?
faqs.answer: All concrete cracks, but a properly installed patio cracks at the control joints — which is by design. The American Concrete Institute calls for control joints cut at intervals 24-30 times the slab thickness (8-10 feet on a 4-inch patio). Cracks outside the joints, cracks wider than 1/8 inch within the first 12 months, or cracks with vertical displacement indicate subgrade or mix-design failure.

faqs.question: How long does it take to install a concrete patio?
faqs.answer: A typical 12x16 patio takes 2-3 days from form-setting to cure-in. Day 1: layout, excavation, base, and forms. Day 2: pour, finish, and joint cuts. Day 3+: curing — surface foot traffic at 24 hours, full design strength at 28 days. Furniture can go on at day 7.

faqs.question: What is the cheapest way to build a concrete patio?
faqs.answer: The lowest legitimate installed price is a 4-inch broom-finish slab on flat ground with direct truck-chute access — that combination lands near $6 per square foot in low-cost markets. Quotes below $5 per square foot usually mean the contractor is skipping reinforcement, control joints, or proper subgrade compaction. Each shortcut shows up as cracking, settlement, or surface failure within 3-7 years.

faqs.question: Is concrete cheaper than pavers for a patio?
faqs.answer: Concrete is meaningfully cheaper than pavers in 2026. Pavers run $15-$30 per square foot installed for natural stone or premium concrete pavers, and $11-$20 for basic concrete pavers. Poured concrete sits at $6-$15. Over a 25-year lifecycle, the cost gap stays in concrete's favor because paver settling and re-leveling work is a recurring cost that poured concrete avoids.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for a concrete patio?
faqs.answer: Most municipalities require a permit for any patio over 200 square feet or any patio attached to a structure. According to the International Code Council, structural code applies when a patio is connected to a load-bearing wall or supports a roof. A freestanding patio under 200 square feet usually does not need a permit. Confirm with the local building department — fines for unpermitted work run $250-$2,500.

howToSteps.text: Topsoil is stripped to 6 inches below final grade. The exposed soil is compacted with a plate compactor. Soft or organic spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.

howToSteps.text: A 3-4 inch compacted layer of crushed stone is installed. 2x4 dimensional lumber is staked along the perimeter to set the slab edges and elevation.

howToSteps.text: 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh is set on chairs at mid-slab depth. On patios that will hold heavy loads, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers is used instead.

howToSteps.name: Pour and finish the slab
howToSteps.text: 3,500 PSI ready-mix is placed via truck chute, pump, or wheelbarrow depending on access. The crew screeds, bull-floats, and applies the final finish (broom, stamped, exposed aggregate).

howToSteps.text: Control joints are cut at 8-10 foot intervals with a walk-behind saw, typically within 6-12 hours of the pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of the slab thickness (1 inch on a 4-inch patio).

howToSteps.name: Cure the slab
howToSteps.text: A curing compound is sprayed on the surface or the slab is covered with plastic and kept damp for 7 days. Foot traffic at 24 hours, furniture at day 7, full strength at day 28.

content:
Quick Answer: A poured concrete patio costs $6 to $15 per square foot installed in 2026. National typical for a 4-inch broom-finish slab is $8.50 per square foot — about $1,650 on a 12x16 patio. Stamped finishes push to $10-$15 per square foot, or $1,900-$2,900 on the same footprint. Backyards without truck access add $1.50-$3 per square foot in pump or wheelbarrow labor.

Concrete patios are the most common decorative concrete project in the United States — and the project where homeowners are most likely to get a vague, lump-sum quote that hides $1,000-$2,000 of variability. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting, so the dollar figures on every page reflect what the slab actually cost to put in the ground. This guide breaks 2026 patio pricing down to per-square-foot, by finish, by size, and by what is moving the number on your quote.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed patio, driveway, slab, and foundation prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. A poured concrete patio in 2026 lands between $6 and $15 per square foot installed, with the national typical near $8.50 per square foot for a 4-inch broom-finish slab on a prepared subgrade. That figure is normalized for 3,500 PSI ready-mix delivery, 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh, control joints at 8-10 foot spacing, and a single mobilization fee. Stamped patios with a single color land between $11 and $14 per square foot, and multi-color stamped finishes push to $13-$18. The single biggest swing factor on a homeowner's actual bill is access — backyards with no truck access add $1.50-$3 per square foot in pump or wheelbarrow labor.

National cost by patio size

Patio pricing scales with square footage, but not linearly — mobilization and minimum-yard surcharges weigh more on small patios, which is why the per-square-foot rate drops as a patio gets bigger. The table below shows 2026 national ranges for common residential patio sizes in standard 4-inch broom finish, including ready-mix, labor, base prep, mesh reinforcement, and a single mobilization fee.

SizeSquare feetLowNational typicalHigh
Small (10' x 10')100 sq ft$650$950$1,400
Standard (12' x 16')192 sq ft$1,150$1,650$2,900
Mid (15' x 20')300 sq ft$1,800$2,550$4,500
Large (20' x 25')500 sq ft$3,000$4,250$7,500
Extra-large (25' x 40')1,000 sq ft$6,000$8,500$15,000

Stamped finishes on the same footprints add roughly 40-60 percent. A 300-square-foot stamped patio lands between $3,300 and $5,400 vs $1,800-$4,500 for broom on the same slab. Service-specific pricing detail is broken down by region on the Cost of Concrete concrete patios service hub.

What drives the per-square-foot price

Five inputs explain almost every dollar of variance on a patio quote. In order of impact: access, finish, base prep, slab thickness, and reinforcement. A homeowner who reads a quote against these five inputs can identify what is missing in 60 seconds.

Access

Truck-chute pours are the cheapest. If the ready-mix truck can park within 20 feet of the patio and chute concrete directly, labor stays at baseline. If the patio is behind a house with no gate access, the concrete has to be pumped or wheelbarrowed in — that is real labor, billed at $1.50-$3 per square foot extra. Quotes that don't address access usually under-price the wheelbarrow scenario.

Finish

Broom finish is the baseline. Each step up the finish ladder adds labor and material:

  • Broom — $0 upcharge, baseline
  • Integral color — +$1 to $2 per square foot (color added at the truck or hand-broadcast)
  • Exposed aggregate — +$2 to $4 per square foot
  • Stamped single-color — +$4 to $6 per square foot
  • Stamped multi-color — +$6 to $9 per square foot
  • Acid-stained or polished — +$3 to $7 per square foot

Base prep

A patio needs a compacted gravel base over compacted subgrade. Light prep on flat, well-drained ground takes a few hours and adds $1-$2 per square foot. Heavy prep — clay soil, slope correction, drainage tile, removal of an existing slab — adds $3-$6 per square foot.

Slab thickness

4 inches is residential standard. Patios under a hot tub, outdoor kitchen, or pizza oven should go to 5 inches. Each inch adds $0.75-$1.25 per square foot. Going thinner than 4 inches saves a small amount up front and routinely cracks within 2-5 years.

Reinforcement

6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh is residential standard for patios. It adds $0.40-$0.60 per square foot. According to the American Concrete Institute, mesh or rebar reinforcement does not prevent cracking — it holds the slab together after a crack forms. Patios without any reinforcement are a structural shortcut that shows up within 5 years.

How to read a concrete patio quote

A complete patio quote names eight items: square footage, slab thickness, PSI of the ready-mix, reinforcement type, control-joint spacing, finish, demolition scope (if any), and access notes. Quotes that skip any of those items are not necessarily dishonest — but the homeowner has no way to compare them against another quote line for line.

Common gaps in residential patio quotes: no PSI listed (assume 3,000 — under-spec for a freeze-thaw region), no reinforcement listed (assume none), no joint spacing listed (cracking risk goes up), and no access plan (wheelbarrow surcharge gets added mid-project as a change order). According to the National Ready Mixed Concrete Association, the minimum recommended mix for residential exterior concrete in freeze-thaw regions is 3,500-4,000 PSI with air entrainment.

How a concrete patio gets installed

The pour is one day. The full project is two to four days. The steps below match what a professional crew executes on a standard residential patio.

  1. Lay out and mark the patio. The contractor stakes the perimeter, confirms square corners with the 3-4-5 method, and verifies a minimum 1/4 inch per foot slope away from the house for drainage.
  2. Excavate and compact the subgrade. Topsoil is stripped to 6 inches below final grade. The exposed soil is compacted with a plate compactor. Soft or organic spots are dug out and replaced with compacted gravel.
  3. Install gravel base and forms. A 3-4 inch compacted layer of crushed stone is installed. 2x4 dimensional lumber is staked along the perimeter to set the slab edges and elevation.
  4. Place wire mesh or rebar. 6-by-6 W2.9 welded wire mesh is set on chairs at mid-slab depth. On patios that will hold heavy loads, #4 rebar on 18-inch centers is used instead.
  5. Pour and finish the slab. 3,500 PSI ready-mix is placed via truck chute, pump, or wheelbarrow depending on access. The crew screeds, bull-floats, and applies the final finish (broom, stamped, exposed aggregate).
  6. Cut control joints. Control joints are cut at 8-10 foot intervals with a walk-behind saw, typically within 6-12 hours of the pour. Joint depth is 1/4 of the slab thickness (1 inch on a 4-inch patio).
  7. Cure the slab. A curing compound is sprayed on the surface or the slab is covered with plastic and kept damp for 7 days. Foot traffic at 24 hours, furniture at day 7, full strength at day 28.

Finish pricing: broom vs stamped vs exposed aggregate

Finish is the single biggest discretionary line item on a patio quote. Every finish below is built on the same 4-inch reinforced slab — structural performance is identical, the upcharge is labor and material for the surface.

Finish2026 price per sq ftUpcharge vs broomBest fit
Broom finish$6 - $10baselinestandard backyard patio
Integral color (broom)$7 - $12+$1 - $2cheapest visual upgrade
Exposed aggregate$8 - $14+$2 - $4slip resistance, pool-deck areas
Acid-stained / polished$9 - $17+$3 - $7covered patios, indoor-outdoor flow
Stamped (single color)$10 - $14+$4 - $6flagstone, cobblestone, slate looks
Stamped (multi-color)$12 - $18+$6 - $9realistic stone, antiqued finishes

For a side-by-side on stamped patterns and color combinations, the stamped and decorative concrete hub on Cost of Concrete has per-pattern price ranges by region.

Concrete patio vs pavers vs flagstone

Concrete is the budget-to-mid-tier option in the patio category. Pavers and flagstone deliver visual variety but at meaningfully higher installed cost and higher lifetime maintenance.

Material2026 installed priceLifespanMaintenance
Poured concrete (broom)$6 - $10 / sq ft30-50 yearsseal every 3-5 years
Poured concrete (stamped)$10 - $15 / sq ft25-40 yearsseal every 2-3 years
Concrete pavers$11 - $20 / sq ft25-40 yearspolymer sand top-up, re-leveling
Natural stone / flagstone$18 - $35 / sq ft40+ yearsperiodic re-set, joint refill
Brick$15 - $25 / sq ft25-50 yearsmortar repointing, moss removal

Lifecycle math usually favors poured concrete. Pavers and flagstone require periodic re-leveling as the base settles, and the labor for that work is rarely included in the initial comparison. The U.S. Census Bureau American Housing Survey shows poured concrete is the most common patio material on owner-occupied homes by a wide margin — installed price and durability are the two reasons.

Regional and seasonal variation

Patio prices vary 25-45 percent between U.S. metros. Cheapest installed pricing in 2026 sits in the Midwest and lower South — Indianapolis, Memphis, Birmingham, Oklahoma City — where ready-mix is locally produced and labor markets are softer. Most expensive markets are Northeast, Pacific Northwest, and California metros, where mix-design surcharges for freeze-thaw or high labor rates push installed prices 30-50 percent above national typical.

Season matters within a single market. Backyard patio crews are busiest April through October. Quotes pulled November-February in non-freezing regions often run 5-10 percent lower because the crew has open capacity. Homeowners can compare local installed pricing on the national best concrete contractors directory, or jump to a regional example like Mooresville, NC to see how a single market prices the same patio scope.

Frequently asked questions

What is the average cost of a concrete patio in 2026?

The national average for a concrete patio in 2026 is $8.50 per square foot installed for a standard 4-inch broom-finish slab. A typical 12x16 patio (192 square feet) costs $1,150 to $2,900 installed in plain finish, and $2,100 to $3,500 in stamped. The largest variable on a backyard patio quote is access — a wheelbarrow pour adds $1.50-$3 per square foot vs a direct truck-chute pour.

Stamped concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot over broom finish — roughly $700-$1,500 on a typical 12x16 patio. According to the Portland Cement Association, the structural performance is identical: the upcharge is entirely labor (extra crew hours during finishing), stamping mats, color hardener, and release agent. The return shows up in resale value, not in concrete performance.

A residential concrete patio should be a minimum of 4 inches thick. Patios that will hold a hot tub, outdoor kitchen, or heavy furniture should bump to 5 inches. Each additional inch of thickness adds about $0.75-$1.25 per square foot. Going below 4 inches saves a few hundred dollars but routinely cracks within 2-5 years.

All concrete cracks, but a properly installed patio cracks at the control joints — which is by design. The American Concrete Institute calls for control joints cut at intervals 24-30 times the slab thickness (8-10 feet on a 4-inch patio). Cracks outside the joints, cracks wider than 1/8 inch within the first 12 months, or cracks with vertical displacement indicate subgrade or mix-design failure.

A typical 12x16 patio takes 2-3 days from form-setting to cure-in. Day 1: layout, excavation, base, and forms. Day 2: pour, finish, and joint cuts. Day 3+: curing — surface foot traffic at 24 hours, full design strength at 28 days. Furniture can go on at day 7.

The lowest legitimate installed price is a 4-inch broom-finish slab on flat ground with direct truck-chute access — that combination lands near $6 per square foot in low-cost markets. Quotes below $5 per square foot usually mean the contractor is skipping reinforcement, control joints, or proper subgrade compaction. Each shortcut shows up as cracking, settlement, or surface failure within 3-7 years.

Concrete is meaningfully cheaper than pavers in 2026. Pavers run $15-$30 per square foot installed for natural stone or premium concrete pavers, and $11-$20 for basic concrete pavers. Poured concrete sits at $6-$15. Over a 25-year lifecycle, the cost gap stays in concrete's favor because paver settling and re-leveling work is a recurring cost that poured concrete avoids.

Do I need permits for a concrete patio?

Most municipalities require a permit for any patio over 200 square feet or any patio attached to a structure. According to the International Code Council, structural code applies when a patio is connected to a load-bearing wall or supports a roof. A freestanding patio under 200 square feet usually does not need a permit. Confirm with the local building department — fines for unpermitted work run $250-$2,500.

Key takeaways

  • National typical for a 12x16 broom-finish concrete patio in 2026 is $1,650, with a real range of $1,150-$2,900.
  • Per-square-foot installed pricing: $6-$10 for broom, $8-$14 for exposed aggregate, $10-$15 for stamped.
  • Access is the largest hidden variable — a wheelbarrow pour adds $1.50-$3 per square foot vs a truck-chute pour.
  • 3,500 PSI ready-mix, 6-by-6 W2.9 wire mesh, and 8-10 foot control joint spacing are the minimum residential specs.
  • Stamped finishes return more in resale value than in concrete performance — the slab underneath is the same.
  • Lifecycle cost favors poured concrete over pavers because paver re-leveling is a recurring expense.

Need a contractor whose patio quote matches these numbers? Cost of Concrete lists vetted contractors by city, sourced from real homeowner-reported pricing. Browse the national directory at best concrete contractors or open your city's page to compare local patio pricing line by line against the national ranges above.
